info.topictitle: B4.2.13 Competitive exclusion and the uniqueness of ecological niches

markdown:
## Why Two Species Can’t Share the Same Niche: The Competitive Exclusion Principle


1. In every ecosystem, **species compete** for limited resources such as **food, water, space, and light**. 
2. If two species depend on **the same resource in the same way**, one will eventually **outcompete the other**. 
3. This concept is known as the **competitive exclusion principle.**

<definition term="Competitive exclusion principle ">Two species **cannot **coexist indefinitely in the same ecological niche if resources are limiting.</definition>

![Niche ](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/3295f77da488a94a577e8a679669f3da6dec5700-685x531.png)

## Experimental Evidence: Tribolium Beetles


1. Classic experiments with **flour beetles** (_Tribolium castaneum_ and _Tribolium confusum_) illustrate competitive exclusion.
2. When raised **separately**, both species thrived across a variety of temperature and humidity conditions.
3. When raised **together**, one species consistently outcompeted the other, depending on environmental conditions.

<callout type="tip">**Key Insight**: The ability to **outcompete rivals is context-dependent, **small environmental shifts can alter competitive outcomes.</callout>

<callout type="self_review">* How does competitive exclusion influence biodiversity in an ecosystem?
* Can you think of an example where human activity has disrupted the niches of species, leading to competitive exclusion?</callout>
